local_names.romanized_local: Durian Belanda (Malay); Hong Mao Liu Lian (Chinese)

region_of_origin: Tropical Americas; widely cultivated in Southeast Asia including Malaysia and Singapore

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 415570002 refers to the plant substance Annona muricata; used as closest parent for the edible fruit.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of fresh soursop flesh is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a generous cup of pulp or a medium wedge of the fruit.

nutrient_source.title: Soursop, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167762)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw soursop (Annona muricata) used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent the edible flesh per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for soursop was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 42 is estimated based on the GI values of analogous tropical fruits with similar sugar composition and fibre content (e.g., custard apple GI ~54, papaya GI ~60, guava GI ~12–31). A conservative mid-range estimate of 42 is applied, consistent with the low-GI classification for high-fibre tropical fruits as documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving = (42 × 16.8 × 1.50) / 100 = 10.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (14 mg/100 g) and high potassium content (278 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Soursop's favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio supports blood pressure management; individuals with hypertension may include it as part of a potassium-rich fruit intake, though portion size should be monitored due to natural sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate natural sugar content (13.5 g/100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (~42)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soursop has a low estimated GI and moderate sugar content; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to approximately 100–150 g per serving and pair with protein or fat to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.05 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and good dietary fibre (3.3 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Soursop is a low-fat, cholesterol-free fruit with meaningful dietary fibre; regular moderate consumption is consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern, though its natural sugar content warrants attention in individuals managing triglycerides.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soursop (Annona muricata), known in Malay as Durian Belanda and in Chinese as 红毛榴莲 (Hong Mao Liu Lian), is a large tropical fruit native to the Americas that has been widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, including Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. The fruit has a spiny green exterior and a white, fibrous, creamy-textured flesh with a distinctive flavour that is simultaneously sweet and tart, often described as a combination of strawberry, pineapple, and citrus with a creamy undertone. In Singapore and Malaysia, soursop is commonly consumed fresh, as a chilled juice or blended drink, or as a dessert ingredient. Nutritionally, soursop provides approximately 66 kcal per 100 g, with 16.8 g of carbohydrate, 1.0 g of protein, and only 0.3 g of fat. It is a good source of dietary fibre (3.3 g/100 g), vitamin C (20.6 mg/100 g), potassium (278 mg/100 g), and B vitamins including thiamine and niacin. Its low sodium content and high potassium make it favourable for blood pressure management. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 42 places it in the low-GI category, making it a relatively suitable fruit for individuals managing blood glucose, though its natural sugar content (13.5 g/100 g) means portion control remains important for those with Type 2 Diabetes. Soursop is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: Is soursop good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy (exact match for raw soursop). GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable tropical fruits from peer-reviewed GI tables; no direct soursop GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database, reducing confidence from high to medium.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as soursop is a raw fruit with no industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based fruit; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero in raw tropical fruit; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. GI value of 42 is an analogous estimate; no direct soursop GI entry was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimate is based on comparable high-fibre tropical fruits (custard apple, guava, papaya) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The food_group has been corrected from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Fruits' as soursop is a whole tropical fruit. The food_name has been normalised from 'SOUR SOP' to 'Soursop' (canonical English spelling). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported by USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167762 and are not sentinel values.
